content.text: Modern data systems face a fundamental tension: raw data arrives fast and in many shapes, but analytical queries demand structure, performance, and reliability. Data lakes resolve this by decoupling storage from compute, letting you land any data cheaply and query it later with the right engine. Table formats then layer transactional guarantees and query performance on top of that raw storage, turning a chaotic pile of files into something that behaves like a proper database.

is a centralized repository — typically object storage like Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, or Azure Data Lake Storage — where raw data is stored in its native format (JSON, CSV, Parquet, Avro, images, logs, etc.) at massive scale, without requiring a predefined schema.

(sometimes called an open table format) is a specification that defines how data files, metadata, and schema information are organized and tracked on top of that object storage. The table format is what makes a collection of files behave like a database table — with schema enforcement, versioning, ACID transactions, and efficient query planning.
The three dominant open table formats today are:
— designed at Netflix, now the most widely adopted
— designed at Uber, strong focus on streaming upserts
— designed at Databricks, tightly integrated with Spark

Traditional data warehouses are fast and structured but expensive, rigid, and vendor-locked. Traditional data lakes are cheap and flexible but unstructured, unreliable (no transactions), and slow to query. Table formats bridge this gap — they give you:
(query data as it looked yesterday or last month)
(any engine — Spark, Trino, DuckDB, Flink — can read the same files)
architecture — warehouse-quality analytics on lake-scale storage.
How a Data Lake Works (Storage Layer)
Object storage has no concept of directories, tables, or schemas. Everything is a flat key-value store: a key is a path-like string (
), and the value is raw bytes. The "folder" structure is just a naming convention.
is the de facto standard. It organizes data column-by-column rather than row-by-row, which means:
Queries that read only 3 out of 100 columns skip 97% of data physically on disk
Each column is compressed independently (integers compress differently from strings)
Column-level statistics (min, max, null count) are stored in the file footer, enabling predicate pushdown
is row-oriented and preferred for streaming/event data where you write entire records frequently.
is columnar like Parquet, historically used in the Hive ecosystem.
Before table formats, the dominant pattern was Hive-style partitioning: organize files into directories based on column values.
Query engines would list directory paths to find relevant files. This works, but has severe problems:
— S3 list operations are expensive at scale (millions of files)
— a write that creates 50 files can be partially visible to readers mid-write
— file 1 and file 2 could have different columns silently
— deleting a row means rewriting the entire partition file
— streaming writes produce millions of tiny files, killing performance
Table formats solve all of these by introducing a
The core idea is: instead of discovering data by listing directories, a table format maintains an explicit
catalog of exactly which files belong to the table
, along with rich statistics about each file. Writers update the catalog atomically; readers consult the catalog first.
Iceberg is the most important format to understand deeply. Its metadata hierarchy has four levels:

Catalog (Hive Metastore / Glue / Nessie / REST)
  └── points to → current metadata.json
        └── contains → list of snapshot IDs
              └── each snapshot points to → manifest list (.avro)
                    └── manifest list contains → list of manifest files (.avro)
                          └── each manifest file contains → list of data files (.parquet)
                                └── with per-file statistics (min/max/null counts per column)
: The entry point. It maps a table name (
) to the location of the latest
file. Catalogs can be Hive Metastore, AWS Glue, Project Nessie, or a REST catalog.
): A JSON file describing the table's current state — schema, partition spec, list of all snapshots, current snapshot ID, and table properties.
: Represents the table's state at a point in time. Each write operation creates a new snapshot. Old snapshots are retained for time travel.
: An Avro file listing all manifest files that belong to a snapshot, with partition-level statistics (min/max values for each partition column across all files in that manifest).
: An Avro file listing actual data files (Parquet/ORC/Avro), with per-file statistics: row count, column-level min/max values, null counts, and file size. This is the key to fast query planning.
: The actual Parquet files containing your data.
Read current snapshot → find manifest list
Scan manifest list → skip manifests whose partition statistics don't include
For remaining manifests, scan manifest files → skip data files whose column statistics don't overlap with the predicate
Read only the qualifying data files
This multi-level pruning means even a table with 10 million files can resolve a query by reading only a handful of data files, without any expensive directory listing.
Writer creates new data files, new manifest, new snapshot
Writer attempts to atomically swap the catalog pointer from old
(using conditional PUT in S3 or compare-and-swap in a proper catalog)
If another writer already swapped, the write is retried or rejected
This means readers always see a consistent snapshot — they're never mid-write. Readers are completely isolated from writers.
Hudi organizes a table as a series of
directory alongside data files. It has two storage types:
: On every update/delete, the affected Parquet files are rewritten with the changes merged in. Reads are fast (data is always clean), writes are slower and more expensive.
: Updates are written as delta log files (Avro) alongside base Parquet files. Reads merge the delta logs with the base file on the fly. Writes are fast, reads are slightly slower. A background
process periodically merges deltas into new base files.
use cases where you continuously upsert rows from a source database into your lake.
directory alongside data files. Each transaction appends a JSON file to this log (e.g.,
) describing what files were added or removed. Periodically, log entries are compacted into

s3://my-bucket/my-table/
  _delta_log/
    00000000000000000000.json   ← initial commit
    00000000000000000001.json   ← add files
    00000000000000000002.json   ← delete files
    00000000000000000010.checkpoint.parquet  ← checkpoint
  part-00001-abc.snappy.parquet
  part-00002-def.snappy.parquet
Reading a Delta table means replaying the log from the last checkpoint forward to reconstruct the current table state.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    DATA SOURCES                          │
│  Databases │ APIs │ Streaming │ Files │ Logs             │
└────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┘
             │
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              INGESTION / LOADING LAYER                   │
│  Python scripts │ Kafka/Flink │ Fivetran │ Airbyte       │
│                                                          │
│  Landing Zone (raw files, no schema enforcement)         │
│  s3://bucket/raw/events/2024-01-15/file.json             │
└────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┘
             │
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              TABLE FORMAT LAYER (Iceberg/Delta/Hudi)     │
│                                                          │
│  Bronze (raw)  →  Silver (cleaned)  →  Gold (aggregated) │
│  (Medallion Architecture)                                │
│                                                          │
│  Metadata: catalog, snapshots, manifests, statistics     │
│  Data: Parquet files on object storage                   │
└────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┘
             │
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              COMPUTE / QUERY LAYER                       │
│  Spark │ Trino │ DuckDB │ Athena │ Snowflake │ Flink     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
is the standard way to organize data in a lakehouse:
: Raw data as-is from sources. Append-only. Preserves history.
: Business-level aggregates, metrics, and mart tables. Query-ready.
Working with Data Lakes in Python
Python is the primary language for building data pipelines that load and transform data in a lakehouse. The key libraries are:
to write Parquet files to S3.

import pandas as pd
import pyarrow as pa
import pyarrow.parquet as pq
import boto3
from datetime import datetime

# Sample data
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "user_id": [1, 2, 3],
    "event": ["click", "view", "purchase"],
    "event_ts": pd.to_datetime(["2024-01-15 10:00", "2024-01-15 10:01", "2024-01-15 10:02"]),
    "amount": [0.0, 0.0, 49.99],
})

# Write to S3 using PyArrow (handles S3 natively via s3fs)
import s3fs

fs = s3fs.S3FileSystem()
table = pa.Table.from_pandas(df)

# Write with Hive-style partitioning
pq.write_to_dataset(
    table,
    root_path="s3://my-bucket/raw/events",
    partition_cols=["event"],  # creates event=click/, event=view/ etc.
    filesystem=fs,
    compression="snappy",
)
This gives you raw Parquet on S3 but no table format guarantees.
is the official Python library for reading and writing Iceberg tables without needing Spark.

from pyiceberg.catalog import load_catalog
from pyiceberg.schema import Schema
from pyiceberg.types import (
    NestedField, LongType, StringType, TimestampType, DoubleType
)
from pyiceberg.partitioning import PartitionSpec, PartitionField
from pyiceberg.transforms import DayTransform
import pyarrow as pa

# Connect to a REST catalog (e.g., AWS Glue, Tabular, Nessie)
catalog = load_catalog(
    "my_catalog",
    **{
        "type": "rest",
        "uri": "https://my-catalog-endpoint.example.com",
        "credential": "my-token",
        "warehouse": "s3://my-bucket/warehouse",
    }
)

# Define schema
schema = Schema(
    NestedField(1, "user_id", LongType(), required=True),
    NestedField(2, "event", StringType(), required=False),
    NestedField(3, "event_ts", TimestampType(), required=True),
    NestedField(4, "amount", DoubleType(), required=False),
)

# Define partitioning by day on event_ts
partition_spec = PartitionSpec(
    PartitionField(
        source_id=3,           # field ID of event_ts
        field_id=1000,
        transform=DayTransform(),
        name="event_date",
    )
)

# Create table (idempotent)
if not catalog.table_exists("db.events"):
    table = catalog.create_table(
        identifier="db.events",
        schema=schema,
        partition_spec=partition_spec,
        location="s3://my-bucket/warehouse/db/events",
    )
else:
    table = catalog.load_table("db.events")

# Write data
arrow_table = pa.table({
    "user_id": pa.array([1, 2, 3], type=pa.int64()),
    "event": pa.array(["click", "view", "purchase"], type=pa.string()),
    "event_ts": pa.array(
        [datetime(2024, 1, 15, 10, 0), datetime(2024, 1, 15, 10, 1), datetime(2024, 1, 15, 10, 2)],
        type=pa.timestamp("us")
    ),
    "amount": pa.array([0.0, 0.0, 49.99], type=pa.float64()),
})

table.append(arrow_table)   # atomic append; creates a new snapshot

# Load the table
table = catalog.load_table("db.events")

# Scan with filters (pushes predicates down to manifest/file level)
from pyiceberg.expressions import GreaterThanOrEqual, LessThan, And

scan = table.scan(
    row_filter=And(
        GreaterThanOrEqual("event_ts", "2024-01-15T00:00:00"),
        LessThan("event_ts", "2024-01-16T00:00:00"),
    ),
    selected_fields=("user_id", "event", "amount"),  # column pruning
    limit=1000,
)

# Convert to Arrow or Pandas
arrow_result = scan.to_arrow()
df = scan.to_pandas()
print(df)

# List all snapshots
for snapshot in table.history():
    print(snapshot.snapshot_id, snapshot.timestamp_ms)

# Read table as of a specific snapshot
old_scan = table.scan(snapshot_id=8170742964295688400)
old_df = old_scan.to_pandas()

# Read table as of a timestamp (milliseconds since epoch)
import time
yesterday_ms = int((time.time() - 86400) * 1000)
historical_scan = table.scan(as_of_timestamp=yesterday_ms)

with table.update_schema() as update:
    update.add_column("session_id", StringType(), doc="Browser session ID")
    # Old files still work — missing column returns NULL
    # No data rewrite needed
(logical delete): Write a "delete file" that says "delete all rows where user_id = 42". The actual data file is untouched. The engine merges deletes at read time.
: Write a file that specifies exact
: Rewrite the affected data files with the rows removed.

# Using PySpark + Iceberg for DML (PyIceberg DML support is growing)
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .config("spark.jars.packages", "org.apache.iceberg:iceberg-spark-runtime-3.5_2.12:1.5.0") \
    .config("spark.sql.extensions", "org.apache.iceberg.spark.extensions.IcebergSparkSessionExtensions") \
    .config("spark.sql.catalog.my_catalog", "org.apache.iceberg.spark.SparkCatalog") \
    .config("spark.sql.catalog.my_catalog.type", "glue") \
    .getOrCreate()

# MERGE INTO (upsert) — core to CDC pipelines
spark.sql("""
    MERGE INTO my_catalog.db.events AS target
    USING updates AS source
    ON target.user_id = source.user_id AND target.event_ts = source.event_ts
    WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET *
    WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *
""")

# DELETE
spark.sql("DELETE FROM my_catalog.db.events WHERE event_ts < '2023-01-01'")
Writing Delta Lake Tables in Python

from deltalake import DeltaTable, write_deltalake
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "user_id": [1, 2, 3],
    "event": ["click", "view", "purchase"],
    "amount": [0.0, 0.0, 49.99],
})

# Write (creates table if not exists)
write_deltalake(
    "s3://my-bucket/delta/events",
    df,
    mode="append",              # or "overwrite"
    partition_by=["event"],
    storage_options={"AWS_REGION": "us-east-1"},
)

# Read
dt = DeltaTable("s3://my-bucket/delta/events", storage_options={"AWS_REGION": "us-east-1"})
df = dt.to_pandas()

# Time travel
dt_old = DeltaTable("s3://my-bucket/delta/events", version=0)

# Schema evolution
write_deltalake(
    "s3://my-bucket/delta/events",
    new_df,
    mode="append",
    schema_mode="merge",        # merges new columns into existing schema
)
Building a Full Bronze → Silver Pipeline in Python

import pyarrow as pa
import pyarrow.compute as pc
from pyiceberg.catalog import load_catalog
from pyiceberg.expressions import GreaterThan
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

catalog = load_catalog("glue", type="glue", region_name="us-east-1")

def run_bronze_to_silver(run_date: datetime):
    """
    Reads yesterday's raw events from Bronze,
    cleans and deduplicates them,
    writes to Silver Iceberg table.
    """
    bronze = catalog.load_table("bronze.events")
    silver = catalog.load_table("silver.events_clean")

    # Read only the day we're processing (partition pruning kicks in)
    cutoff = run_date - timedelta(days=1)
    scan = bronze.scan(
        row_filter=GreaterThan("event_ts", cutoff.isoformat()),
        selected_fields=("user_id", "event", "event_ts", "amount"),
    )
    raw = scan.to_arrow()

    # --- Transformations ---
    # 1. Drop rows with null user_id
    mask = pc.is_valid(raw.column("user_id"))
    clean = raw.filter(mask)

    # 2. Cast amount to float (in case source sent strings)
    clean = clean.set_column(
        clean.schema.get_field_index("amount"),
        "amount",
        pc.cast(clean.column("amount"), pa.float64())
    )

    # 3. Deduplicate by (user_id, event_ts) keeping last
    import pandas as pd
    df = clean.to_pandas()
    df = df.sort_values("event_ts").drop_duplicates(
        subset=["user_id", "event_ts"], keep="last"
    )
    clean = pa.Table.from_pandas(df, schema=clean.schema)

    # 4. Append to Silver (atomic)
    silver.append(clean)
    print(f"Written {len(clean)} rows to silver.events_clean for {run_date.date()}")

run_bronze_to_silver(datetime.now())

One operational reality of table formats is that small files accumulate over time — especially from streaming writes or frequent small appends. Small files hurt query performance because each file requires a metadata fetch and a separate read operation.
merges many small files into fewer large files (target: 128MB–512MB per file).

# Iceberg compaction via PySpark
spark.sql("""
    CALL my_catalog.system.rewrite_data_files(
        table => 'db.events',
        strategy => 'binpack',
        options => map('target-file-size-bytes', '536870912')  -- 512MB
    )
""")

# Expire old snapshots (reclaim storage)
spark.sql("""
    CALL my_catalog.system.expire_snapshots(
        table => 'db.events',
        older_than => TIMESTAMP '2024-01-01 00:00:00',
        retain_last => 5
    )
""")

# Remove orphan files (files not tracked by any snapshot)
spark.sql("""
    CALL my_catalog.system.remove_orphan_files(table => 'db.events')
""")
In Delta Lake, the equivalent operations are

: Writing Iceberg tables without a proper catalog (using a Hadoop catalog on local disk) works in development but is not atomic or concurrent-safe in production. Always use a real catalog (Glue, Nessie, REST).

: Partitioning by day is often right. Partitioning by hour creates too many partitions and too many small files. Partitioning by month makes individual partitions too large and reduces pruning effectiveness. The rule is: each partition file should be 128MB–1GB.
with millions of users creates millions of partitions — one file per user. This kills performance. Partition on low-cardinality columns (date, region, event type).
: Never running compaction means performance degrades steadily as small files accumulate. Schedule compaction as a regular maintenance job.
: Two engines (Spark and DuckDB) trying to write to the same Iceberg table without going through a shared catalog will corrupt the table because they can't coordinate snapshot commits.
Treating the lake like a data warehouse
: Loading all data into one giant flat table with no clear Bronze/Silver/Gold structure makes the lake unmanageable. Always apply Medallion architecture from the start.
: Old snapshots and their orphan files remain on S3 indefinitely unless you run
: Rather than exposing partition columns to users (which forces users to filter on
). The engine handles predicate pushdown automatically.
: Files in this range balance parallelism (many engines can read them in parallel) with per-file overhead (metadata reads, S3 GET requests). Use compaction to enforce this.
: Z-ordering co-locates rows with similar values across multiple columns in the same files. If you frequently filter by
together, Z-ordering on both columns dramatically reduces files scanned.

-- Delta Lake Z-order
OPTIMIZE events ZORDER BY (user_id, event_date)

-- Iceberg equivalent (sort-based rewrite)
CALL catalog.system.rewrite_data_files(
    table => 'db.events',
    strategy => 'sort',
    sort_order => 'user_id ASC NULLS LAST, event_date ASC NULLS LAST'
)
call is a transaction and creates a snapshot. Writing one row at a time creates millions of snapshots and manifest files. Batch at least thousands of rows per write, ideally 100K+.
: Never modify Bronze data. It is your audit log and source of truth. All cleaning happens in Silver. This lets you reprocess Silver from scratch if logic changes.

: In Iceberg, field identity is tracked by numeric field ID, not name. This means you can rename a column safely. But never reuse an old field ID for a different column — this breaks backward compatibility with older readers.
: New greenfield projects, multi-engine environments, cloud-native setups on AWS (Glue catalog), need for partition evolution.
: Already on Databricks or Azure, want the simplest setup with no catalog dependency.
: Heavy CDC workloads (continuous upserts from operational databases), streaming ingestion with low-latency requirements.
: AWS Glue Data Catalog, Project Nessie (Git-for-data, supports branching), Apache Polaris (Iceberg REST catalog, now open-sourced by Snowflake), Unity Catalog (Databricks).

: Apache Spark (most feature-complete), Trino/Presto (fast interactive SQL), DuckDB (in-process analytics on Parquet/Iceberg/Delta, excellent for local development), AWS Athena (serverless S3 queries), Snowflake (Iceberg external tables), Apache Flink (streaming reads/writes).
— Rust-backed Delta Lake Python bindings, no JVM required
— Arrow columnar format, the backbone of Python data lake work
— in-process SQL over Parquet, Iceberg, and Delta without Spark
: Apache Airflow, Prefect, Dagster — all commonly used to schedule Bronze → Silver → Gold pipelines.
: Airbyte, Fivetran (managed CDC), Debezium (open-source CDC from databases), Kafka Connect, AWS DMS.

is the most important performance mechanism. A well-maintained Iceberg table with good column statistics and Z-ordering can skip 99%+ of data files for selective queries — making a 10 PB table query in seconds.

becomes a concern at extreme scale. A table with 100 million data files will have huge manifest files. Iceberg's manifest list / manifest file two-level structure mitigates this, but regular compaction (merging manifests via

are handled by optimistic concurrency. Under high write throughput (hundreds of writers per second), conflict rates increase and retries add latency. For these workloads, Hudi's timeline-based locking or Delta Lake's optimistic concurrency with retry is better tuned.

typically writes micro-batches every 1–5 minutes. Each micro-batch creates new files. Without compaction, this creates thousands of files per day. Schedule compaction to run every few hours to maintain file sizing targets.

on large Iceberg tables still require reading metadata. For extremely large tables (millions of data files), pre-warming the metadata layer through a catalog with caching (Polaris, Nessie) significantly reduces planning time.
is protected by S3 server-side encryption (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS) or equivalent on other clouds. Iceberg does not add its own encryption layer — it relies on the storage layer.
can be implemented via Iceberg's row filter and column mask capabilities in supporting catalogs (Unity Catalog, Polaris). This lets you define: "user group A can only see rows where

: Because Iceberg commits are atomic (all-or-nothing catalog swap), a writer that crashes mid-write leaves orphan data files that are never committed to any snapshot. These are safe — readers never see them — but they consume storage until
: Readers hold a snapshot reference for the duration of their query. Even if writers commit 10 new snapshots during a long-running query, the reader sees a consistent point-in-time view.
, timestamp, and the operation type. Combined with snapshot expiry policies, this gives you a configurable audit window.

: Because data is on durable object storage (S3 provides 11 nines durability) and the full history is preserved in snapshots, recovering from accidental deletes is as simple as rolling back to a previous snapshot — no backup/restore process needed.
The combination of cheap object storage, open table formats, and Python's rich data ecosystem makes the lakehouse pattern the dominant architecture for modern analytical data platforms at any scale.
